response.evidence: The data shows systematic variation in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), then Black or African American applicants (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants (64.8-75.3%). Notably, Asian applicants also request higher average loan amounts ($522k-$598k) and have higher incomes ($176k-$209k) compared to other groups, while Black applicants request lower amounts ($397k-$429k) with lower incomes ($135k-$171k). However, the 20+ percentage point gap in acceptance rates between Asian (highest) and some Black/Native groups (lowest) persists even when comparing similar demographic segments, suggesting factors beyond just loan amount and income may influence outcomes.

The data shows systematic disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American (64.8-73.0%) and American Indian or Alaska Native (64.8-75.3%) applicants have notably lower acceptance rates. These differences persist even when comparing groups with similar financial profiles. For example, Asian Males (Non-Hispanic) have 87.8% acceptance with avg income $209K and avg loan $598K, while Black Males (Non-Hispanic) have 70.5% acceptance with avg income $160K and avg loan $429K. However, when comparing White Males (Hispanic) with 79.2% acceptance (avg income $150K, avg loan $395K) to Black Males (Hispanic) with 64.8% acceptance (avg income $172K, avg loan $403K), the Black applicants actually have higher income but lower acceptance rates. This pattern of lower acceptance rates for Black and Native American applicants relative to their financial metrics suggests potential racial disparities in loan approval decisions.

The data shows systematic differences in acceptance rates across racial groups with similar financial profiles. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), then Black or African American applicants (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants (64.8-75.3%). Notably, Asian Male (Not Hispanic) applicants have an 87.8% acceptance rate with average loan amount $598K and income $209K, while Black Male (Not Hispanic) applicants have 70.5% acceptance rate with comparable average loan amount $429K and income $160K. The debt-to-income and loan-to-value ratios are similar across groups (43-44% and 56-71% respectively), suggesting similar financial risk profiles. This pattern of acceptance rate disparity across racial categories, holding other financial metrics relatively constant, constitutes statistical evidence of differential treatment by race.

The data shows consistent patterns of differential acceptance rates across racial groups with similar financial profiles. Asian applicants have acceptance rates of 86.5-87.8%, White applicants 75.9-84.5%, while Black or African American applicants have rates of 64.8-73.0%, and American Indian/Alaska Native applicants 64.8-75.3%. Notably, Asian Male Non-Hispanic applicants have an 87.8% acceptance rate with average loan amount $598,379 and income $209K, while Black Male Non-Hispanic applicants have 70.5% acceptance rate with comparable average loan amount $429,232 and income $160K. The acceptance rate gap of 17+ percentage points between Asian and Black applicants, and 14+ percentage points between White and Black applicants, persists across gender and ethnicity combinations, suggesting systematic differences in outcomes by race even when financial metrics (loan amounts, income, debt-to-income ratios which are similar across groups at ~43-44%) are comparable.

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (75.9-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences of 15-23 percentage points between Asian and Black applicants persist even when comparing groups with similar average financial metrics (debt-to-income ratios are consistently 43-44% across all groups, and loan-to-value ratios are comparable at 56-71%). The systematic pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial groups with similar financial profiles suggests differential treatment in loan outcomes.

The data shows systematic variation in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while American Indian/Alaska Native (64.8-75.3%) and Black/African American applicants (64.8-73.0%) have notably lower acceptance rates. This 15-23 percentage point gap persists even though debt-to-income ratios are similar across all groups (43-44%) and loan-to-value ratios are comparable. Asian and White applicants do show higher average incomes and property values, but Black/African American applicants with similar debt ratios face substantially lower acceptance rates, suggesting factors beyond standard financial metrics may be influencing outcomes.

The data shows systematic variation in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American (64.8-73.0%), American Indian or Alaska Native (64.8-75.3%), and Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander (50.0-75.3%) applicants have notably lower acceptance rates. This pattern persists across sex and ethnicity categories. Asian and White applicants also show higher average incomes and loan amounts, but the acceptance rate gaps remain substantial even when comparing groups with similar financial metrics (e.g., debt-to-income ratios are similar across all groups at ~43-44%). The consistent ordering of acceptance rates by race across multiple demographic segments suggests racial disparities in outcomes.

The data shows systematic variation in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White non-Hispanic (83.8-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have lower rates (70.5-73.0%). American Indian/Alaska Native rates vary (66.3-75.3%). These differences persist despite similar debt-to-income ratios across groups (43-44% range) and comparable loan-to-value ratios. Asian and White non-Hispanic applicants also receive higher average loan amounts ($478K-$598K) compared to Black applicants ($398K-$429K) and American Indian applicants ($323K-$348K). The pattern is consistent across gender and ethnicity subgroups, suggesting racial disparities in outcomes that are not fully explained by the financial metrics provided.

The data shows systematic differences in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (75.9-84.5%), while American Indian/Alaska Native (64.8-75.3%) and Black/African American applicants (64.8-73.0%) have notably lower acceptance rates. These differences persist even when Asian and White applicants have similar or sometimes lower average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and comparable loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian Male Non-Hispanic applicants have an 87.8% acceptance rate compared to Black Male Non-Hispanic applicants at 70.5%, despite both groups having similar debt-to-income ratios (43.4% vs 44.0%). This pattern of 10-20 percentage point gaps in acceptance rates across racial categories, holding other financial metrics relatively constant, constitutes statistical evidence of differential outcomes by race.

The data shows systematic differences in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while American Indian/Alaska Native (64.8-75.3%) and Black/African American (64.8-73.0%) applicants have notably lower acceptance rates. These disparities persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios across groups (ranging from 43.4 to 44.9), and Black/African American applicants actually have higher average loan-to-value ratios (65.1-70.3) than some groups with higher acceptance rates. The acceptance rate gap of approximately 15-23 percentage points between the highest and lowest performing groups, with consistent patterns across demographic categories, provides statistical evidence of differential outcomes.
